why: B is the most accurate and complete on GTM wide-IP-to-pool failover, correctly emphasizing pool-level failover and load balancing methods without over-claiming reference detail.
A gives clean, accurate Device Management > Device Groups steps with correct Sync-Failover type and members/traffic-group setup, while B mislabels the K-article and rambles.
B nails the canonical BIG-IP tcpdump syntax (-s0, -ni 0.0:nnn VLAN, -w /shared/tmp) faithful to the K411/K000154563 references with correct citations and no padding.
All are vague on the actual F5OS workflow, but A correctly frames it as system-level VLAN creation then tenant assignment, while C fabricates inapplicable tmsh syntax and conflates layers.
All three correctly identify K000157932 with proper citation; A and C give the full article title making them marginally cleaner.
B adds the accurate detail that multiple pools under a wide IP fail over by priority/order, which is the precise GTM mechanism, edging out the otherwise-equal A and C.

B gives accurate, concise Device Management > Device Groups steps with correct Sync-Failover type, while C invents some menu paths (Device Trust Domain, commit) and A is longer with a couple of imprecise menu names.
A and C use correct BIG-IP interface naming (1.0/0.0) and valid filter syntax, while B recommends '-i any' and writes to /var/tmp with a bogus '-s port 80' filter that is wrong for BIG-IP.
All fabricate F5OS specifics, but C at least keeps tmsh net vlan syntax plausibly tenant-side, whereas A invents a non-existent F5OS 'vlan {}' config block and B wrongly drags in iWorkflow.

All correctly refuse to fabricate a K-number, but C honestly grounds the refusal in its knowledge cutoff (the real reason it can't know a Nov-2025 article) rather than wrongly asserting the CVE doesn't exist.
